figures.caption: Figure 1: Architectural map, theoretical foundations and the dual computational frameworks of the Separable Neural Architecture. Inputs x is projected into localized coordinate subsets S selected by an interaction tensor embedding T = E(C), processed through parallel learnable function blocks – atoms ϕ(S ) – summed and nonlinearity ρ applied to form the approximant f . The interaction tensor is constrained by maximum interactivity k and rank r.
Figure 2: VSNA convergence behaviour. L2 error versus cells C for B-spline subatoms of degree p ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4} (left) and Fourier sine subatoms (right).

Figure 3: VSNA solution of an inviscid Burgers equation. The nonlinear initial-boundary-value problem schematic (left). Filled contours of the recovered solution uVSNA(x, t) (center-left) and exact solution u(x, t) (center-right). Pointwise absolute errors |uVSNA(x, t) − u(x, t)| (right).

Figure 4: Nonlinear VSNA convergence study. Parametric scaling frontier of L2 error against total trainable parameters (left), traced along rankisolines r ∈ {4, 8, 16, 32, 64} with increasing resolution C ∈ {4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128}. The exact solution, derived via Cole-Hopf transformation, and VSNA approximation (from r = 128,C = 32)(top-right) and pointwise absolute error maps for the best solution for each of r = 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 (bottom-right).

Figure 5: Normalized errors. Spatiotemporal evolution of the field for fixed ω = π 3 and D = 0.001. The top and middle rows compare the VSNA solution with the reference, whilst the bottom row shows the normalized pointwise error |uVSNA−uref |

Figure 6: Solution manifold. The six-dimensional spatiotemporal–parametric advection-diffusion field learned by the VSNA. Stacked x-y spatial slices across time t are shown across rotation-diffusivity ω-D parameter space, illustrating recovery of the complete solution manifold within a single global representation.

Figure 7: VSNA scaling. Approximation (L2) error versus trainable parameters for the same system under refinement of rank r and resolution C. Rank-isolines are connected and colour-coded. Along rank-isolines, errors decrease with resolution at slope = −4 before saturating at the rank capacity limit. Across ranks, an efficient frontier emerges (fitted slope ≈ −0.68 in log-log space), sustained across four orders of magnitude in parameter count.
Table 1: Computational comparison: VSNA versus six-dimensional FEM. The C = 94 projection follows from the O(C18) complexity of direct solvers.
Table 2: Regression benchmark on a noisy 20D Sobol-G Function.

Figure 8: Empirical scaling behavior of the SNA on ImageNet CLIP embedding space. (left) Total trainable parameters versus negative loglikelihood (NLL) under rank refinement for fixed C = 2, 4, 8, 16. (right) Isolated power-law scaling fit for the Pareto-dominant C = 2 configuration.

Figure 9: Empirical scaling behavior of SNA classification on MNIST from pretrained latent spaces. Total trainable parameter versus test negative log-likelihood (NLL) (left) under fixed quadratic B-spline resolution C = 4, across expanding dimensions d. Isolated power-law scaling fit (right) for the d = 32 manifold with slope α = −1.18.

Figure 10: Schematic and reference frame for the laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) model. (left) 3D spatial domain and boundary conditions including a preheated inlet, substrate conduction and convection and radiation under a shaped laser beam. (right) Top-down view of the laser-fixed Eulerian frame of reference; the meltpool defined by the T = Tmelt isotherm.

Table 3: Computational comparison between sparse FEM and the VSNA manifold representation. The FEM full-sweep estimate corresponds to serial evaluation over the complete 16 × 16 × 16 × 32 = 131,072 point parameter space. VSNA timings correspond to offline manifold training and online batched inference on a 180 × 96 × 64 spatial grid. All results computed on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU (40GB).

Figure 11: VSNA validation against FEM at nominal parameters. Midline cross-section (left) and top surface (center) of the learned temperature field T queried at v = 0.8m/s, k = 30 W mK , P = 240W and s = 0, a pure Gaussian beam. Pointwise relative errors against a finite-element reference (right).
Table 4: Thermophysical properties, bounding spatial dimensions and parametric manifold search space configurations.

Figure 12: Recovered thermal manifold across process parameter space. Top-surface temperature rise θ queried from the pretrained VSNA across a 3 × 3 grid of low, medium and high scan speeds v ∈ {0.50, 1.25, 2.00}m/s and tool power P ∈ {100, 200, 300}W at fixed k = 27.5 W mK and s = 0.5.

Figure 13: Probabilistic meltpool geometries under process uncertainty. Laser irradiance for the six discrete beam-shape settings s ∈ {0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1} (top). Top-surface (middle) and midline cross-section (bottom) liquidus isotherms extracted from Monte Carlo queries of the pretrained VSNA for the six beam-shape parameters. The black contour denotes the median boundary; the shaded region the interquartile range.

Figure 14: Inverse process maps under uncertainty. Contour maps of the probability (Psuccess) of producing a meltpool with width 130 ± 10µm and depth 55± 5µm for combinations of P ∈ [100, 300]W and v ∈ [0.5, 2.0]m/s. Only for two beam configurations s = 0.2 (left) and s = 0.4 (right) is this feasible. Contours in the expanded insets chart the reliability transitions Psuccess ∈ {0.10, 0.25, 0.50}. Gold stars identify the probability maximizers.

Figure 15: Experimental schematic. A laser directed energy deposition machine builds thin-walled structures layer by layer on a stainless steel 304 substrate, whilst an infrared camera records the evolving thermal field during the build. These measurements are subsequently linked to the mechanical response of the material through tensile testing of extracted coupons.

Figure 16: Comparative performance. Predictive performance versus trainable parameters on the Inconel 718 thermal-history dataset. The SNA matches or exceeds prior model accuracy on both yield stress (YS) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) with up to five orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters [61, 62], and three orders fewer than XGBoost [54].

Figure 17: Generated thermal histories. Generative inversion of three target mechanical properties – yield stress and ultimate tensile stress – to thermal histories, with mean and range illustrated. This signal can be used as the target during in situ monitoring and control of the DED process.
